title: Birdhouse Bath Shelf with Towel Bar
summary: A birdhouse-roofline shelf with a built-in wooden towel bar and a drop shelf for folded towels.
description:
The two side pieces are the one place this build asks for real care: they're cut as true mirror images, both beveled 30° so the roofline peaks upward, and both need matching holes drilled for the towel bar to pass through. Drill the towel-bar holes before you cut the final profile — corner-to-corner on a scrap test piece first, since lining up two holes on two separate boards so a single rod slides through both cleanly is fussier than it looks on paper.

Everything after that is straightforward glue-and-nail assembly: shelf between the sides, a mitered "roof" cap on top, a plywood or beadboard back, and a drop-ledge underneath for a second, lower surface. The optional double robe hooks are worth the small extra step if your bathroom wall space is tight — the larger single hooks shown as an alternative need more clearance than most powder rooms have.

tools.note: Cuts the two side profiles — practice the mirrored bevel-and-hole layout on scrap first.
For the towel-bar holes through both sides.

materials.name: Wooden closet rod, 1" dia
materials.note: The towel bar — cut to fit between the sides.

materials.name: Double robe hooks
materials.note: Optional. Fit tighter wall spaces than the larger single hooks.

cutList.note: Longest-point measurement; top end beveled 30° off square.
Top end beveled 30° off square, longest-point measurement — cut as true mirror images with matching towel-bar holes.
Top end angled 30° off square, longest-point measurement.

steps.title: Cut the two sides as mirror images
steps.body: Cut the two side pieces with a jigsaw, keeping in mind the top bevels must angle upward on BOTH sides — that means the two sides are cut as true mirror images of each other, not identical copies. Drill the towel-bar opening in all four corners with a drill bit, then connect the dots with the jigsaw; this is trickier than it sounds, so practice on a scrap piece first. Sand both jigsaw-cut edges now so they match and are smooth.

steps.title: Cut the remaining parts
steps.body: With the sides shaped, cut the rest of the 1x6: the shelf 22 1/2" and the two roof cap pieces 12 1/2", each with its top end beveled 30° off square. Those 12 1/2" and 16 1/4" figures are longest-point measurements, so set the saw to 30° and read the long face of the board, not the short one. All five 1x6 parts together use about 80" of the 8 ft board, so there is room to recut a bevel if the first one goes wrong. From the 1x2 cut two roof trim pieces 16 1/4" with a 30° top end and two drop ledge pieces 25". Cut the back panel 24" x 13 3/4" from the 1/4" plywood or beadboard.

steps.title: Attach the shelf
steps.body: Stand both sides on edge with their 30° bevels pointing up and away from each other, and set the 22 1/2" shelf between them. That length is deliberate: 22 1/2" plus the two 3/4" sides comes to 24", exactly the width of the back panel, so if the assembly does not measure 24" across the outside the back will not fit. Glue and drive two or three 2" finish nails through the outside of each side into the shelf's end grain, predrilling near the ends so the pine does not split. Check the shelf is square to both sides before the glue sets.

steps.title: Attach the roof cap
steps.body: Mark the roof cap pieces on both their top and underside so you know exactly where and how to line them up. If you've already cut the back plywood, lay it on top of the shelf assembly and use it as an alignment pattern by matching edges. Attach the roof cap with nails and glue, and double-check its angle matches the roof trim you'll add in a later step.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Attach the back panel with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue.

steps.title: Add the roof trim
steps.body: Glue and nail the two 16 1/4" roof trim pieces along the outer edges of the roof cap, their 30° ends meeting at the peak the same way the cap boards do. Dry-fit the pair at the peak first: if the two angled ends leave a gap, shave one with a light pass on the saw rather than forcing the joint closed with nails, because a forced miter opens again as the glue dries. Use 1 1/4" finish nails here — the trim is only 3/4" thick over another 3/4" board, and a 2" nail will come through.

steps.title: Add the drop ledge
steps.body: Glue and nail the two 25" drop ledge pieces beneath the main shelf to form the lower surface for folded towels. At 25" they are 1" longer than the 24" outside width of the case, which is exactly the 1/2" overhang at each end — measure that overhang at both ends before nailing rather than centring by eye. Drive 1 1/4" finish nails up into the shelf and back into the sides so no fastener shows from the front.

steps.title: Install the towel bar
steps.body: Cut the 1" closet rod to 24" so it passes through both sides and finishes flush with their outside faces — 22 1/2" between the sides plus the two 3/4" thicknesses. Slide it through, check it sits level and has not bowed the sides in or out, then glue it in place. Wipe any squeeze-out off the rod before it dries: a bead of hardened glue is exactly where a towel will catch and snag.

steps.title: Add hooks (optional)
steps.body: If you want hanging hooks, mount the double robe hooks on the front face below the shelf, spaced evenly and screwed into the solid 3/4" pine rather than into the 1/4" back. Double hooks fit tighter wall spaces than the larger single hooks shown as an alternative, which is why they are the better choice in a small bathroom. When you hang the finished shelf, drive the mounting screws through the back panel into wall studs, or use anchors rated for the load — a loaded towel bar pulls far harder on a wall than the shelf's own weight suggests.

images.alt: Wall-mounted bathroom shelf with a birdhouse-style peaked roof and a built-in wooden towel bar
